EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary oriented southwest to northeast slowly made its way into and across Iowa from the northwest through the 19th and early into the 20th.  The boundary generally stalled over central Iowa mid morning and into the evening, allowing storms to initiate and train over the same areas much of the day before becoming more progressive later.  Resulting issues were primarily heavy rainfall in excess of 2 inches, along with a couple of severe wind reports and a flash flood report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail also reported. Lasted roughly 3 to 4 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary moved northward into the the state overnight and initiated showers and storms through the morning hours. While no severe weather was reported, heavy rain was experienced by a few, primarily across southern/south-central Iowa. Rainfall amounts generally resided in the 2 to 3 inch range with a few closer to 3.3-3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small funnel cloud, long and rope-like. It never touched down to the ground. Reported near 155th St. and Echo Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms initiated ahead of a cold front through portions of central Iowa, within a warm and moist environment, ripe with dew points into mid and upper 70s.  A couple of the morning thunderstorms were able to produce damaging winds, while thunderstorms associated with the cold front later that evening produced a few more damaging wind reports.  Overall, most damage consisted of tree branches, with a few small trees snapped or uprooted as well.

These thunderstorms produced hail ranging in size from a pea in Lee County to up to the size of nickels in Van Buren County. In addition to the hail, rainfall amounts of 1.50 inches were reported in Van Buren County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary draped itself across the state the previous day and into the 28th, oscillating roughly between northern and southern Iowa.  During the morning and early afternoon of the 28th the boundary had made its way into southern Iowa, allowing storms to initiate and train in the area. Given the thermodynamic and wind profiles, a little bit of everything was able to occur.  Ranging from flash flooding, a funnel cloud, sub-severe hail, and damaging winds. While a bit of everything occurred, it was all very low end by severe weather standards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser reported a small, poorly defined funnel from an elevated storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved into northeast Nebraska during the day on August 23rd, warm and moist air returned northward into the area along a warm front. As temperatures increased into the mid and upper 80s along and south of the warm front, thunderstorms began to develop by late afternoon. These thunderstorms continued to increase in coverage and intensity during the evening. Favorable shear along the warm front allowed for some of the storms to form into supercells and produce severe weather. Late in the evening the thunderstorms formed into a complex and moved southeast across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa with severe weather and heavy rainfall occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs up to 4 inches in diameter were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving southward during the day on the 2nd brought strong, gusty northwest winds to eastern Iowa. Sustained wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts as high as 60 mph caused damage to trees, power lines, and personal property.  The winds were the strongest from 7AM to 7PM CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Cedar Rapids Airport reported a gusts between 40 and 50 knots from 726 AM CST through 1 PM CST. In addition to the measured gusts, Law Enforcement reported that a 3 foot diameter tree fell on the front of an SUV injuring 2 people.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of nocturnal storms blossomed across western and southwest Iowa, remaining relatively stationary for a couple hours before slowly moving eastward through the morning hours. Some redevelopment occurred to the north, before sliding southward and weakening into the afternoon.  In all, heavy rain was the primary result with locations in excess of 4 inches and even a 7 inch report in far southern Iowa. A pair of thunderstorm wind damage reports were associated with more organized linear storm development in west central/northwest Iowa during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported several trees up to one foot in diameter down near Schaller. Time and location estimated from radar and reported via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms initiated ahead of a cold front through portions of central Iowa, within a warm and moist environment, ripe with dew points into mid and upper 70s.  A couple of the morning thunderstorms were able to produce damaging winds, while thunderstorms associated with the cold front later that evening produced a few more damaging wind reports.  Overall, most damage consisted of tree branches, with a few small trees snapped or uprooted as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a 2 foot diameter tree snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of thunderstorms with heavy rains moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the evening of August 23rd into the early morning hours of the 24th. This heavy rain produced flash flooding across portions of Mitchell, Howard, Chickasaw, Winneshiek and Allamakee Counties. One person drowned north of Lawler (Chickasaw County) when a vehicle was swept off the road by flood waters from the Little Turkey River. Two people had to be rescued from a cabin near Lansing (Allamakee County). Flood waters caused a basement to collapsed in Calmar (Winneshiek County) and evacuations were performed east of Decorah (Winneshiek County) because of rapidly rising flood waters along the Upper Iowa River. A Flash Flood Emergency was issued for these extreme events. Numerous roads were covered by flood waters in Mitchell County where sandbagging efforts occurred near Osage. Several schools had to close on the 24th because of the flooding. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties. Damages from the flooding to public infrastructure was estimated at $2.4 million for Winneshiek County and another $1 million for Decorah (Winneshiek County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Elma measured 5.03 inches of rain.

These thunderstorms slowly became organized into a line and produced strong damaging thunderstorms winds as they moved east southeast through eastern Nebraska and into southwest Iowa through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer estimated wind gusts up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over areas of western South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of July 6th. These thunderstorms moved east and developed upscale into a large convective complex that moved into northeast Nebraska during the late evening on the 6th of July. The line of thunderstorms moved southeast across all of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa with the primary severe weather threat being from damaging winds. The thunderstorms exited into northeast Kansas and northern Missouri during the early morning. This complex of thunderstorms then left an outflow boundary across the area, which additional thunderstorms developed along during the afternoon of July 7th.

Thunderstorms develop late in the evening of July 17 in response to the nocturnal strengthening of the low-level jet over a stalled frontal boundary over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. Although the majority of these thunderstorms were strong, only isolated reports of severe weather were observed mainly in the form of strong winds. In addition to the severe weather, the orientation of the thunderstorms from northwest to southeast resulted in repeated or training of the storms and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several county roads were reported flooded across the County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 25th, a cold front progressed into portions of central and eastern Nebraska and eventually stalled out in eastern Nebraska.  During the evening hours storms that initiated earlier in the day in western Nebraska continued to make their way across Nebraska and into Iowa and produced a number of high wind, damaging wind, and hail reports.  Prior to the main line, a severe storm fired up in northern Iowa in Emmet and produced near severe hail and severe winds. After the main line pushed through in the overnight hours, a stalled front in northern Missouri aided in ushering in another round of heavy rainfall across southern Iowa, where reports upwards of 4 plus inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Iowa DOT RWIS Mesonet station 1 mile east of Estherville reported a peak wind gust of 54 kts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary extended from northeast Nebraska through western and into southern Iowa. Nocturnal thunderstorms, which had developed over central Nebraska, moved into southwest Iowa by mid morning and as they approached the front they intensified producing severe weather in Shelby County before moving east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported that a tree fell on a car along Highway 59 just to the north of Harlan. There were no injuries reported in the vehicle.

A funnel cloud was sighted over northeast Iowa during the evening of July 30th. The funnel cloud occurred with showers that were moving slowly across the region and was seen near Rock Creek (Mitchell County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted near Rock Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first push of cold air for the season made its way into Iowa with many locations receiving the first freeze of the fall season.

Two rivers remained in flood until early October across portions of central and northeast Iowa. The Cedar River at Cedar Falls and the Iowa River at Tama Highway E49 remained in flood for a few days in early October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar River at Cedar Falls crested at 98.9 feet on 24 September at 12 pm CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid airmass resided over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa on the evening of July 5th. This led to extreme instability over the area. A cluster of thunderstorms developed over central Nebraska and moved east northeast into this atmosphere. These thunderstorms were response for wind damage, some significant, as they moved across eastern Nebraska and into west central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were reported downed in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions were not very supportive for the development of any severe weather with weak wind profiles, lack of a focusing mechanism, and large CAPE profiles.  However, with SBCAPE around 1000-1500 J/kg (and a couple hundred in the 0-3km layer), light surface winds, and marginally steep low-level lapse rates, a few funnel clouds were reported with weak shower and storm activity across northern Iowa.  Additionally, with very slow moving storms, at least one location reported 2.00 inches of heavy rainfall due to multiple storms developing over the same location near Thornton, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported multiple funnel clouds, with some lasting as long as 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and very humid weather, with daytime temperatures reaching the 95 to 100 degree range, brought the heat index to 100 to 115 degrees during the afternoon hours of July 20th through July 23rd. Emergency rooms at hospitals reported that an unknown number of people suffered heat stress, heat exhaustion, or dehydration, although no lasting heat related illnesses were reported. Dew points in the 70s over the area reflected the very high humidity, with dew points at a few places peaking near 80 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reaching daytime highs of 95 to 100 were accompanied by very humid conditions, with the heat index rising to 100 to 115 degrees. An unknown number of people suffered from heat stress, heat exhaustion, or dehydration, as reported by hospital emergency rooms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance ahead of a storm system in the southern plains spawned a brief, weak tornado with an area of showers and thunderstorms in southeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a brief tornado touchdown with no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary set up across the southwest third of the state through the day of the 25th as surface low pressure developed in eastern Nebraska.  Storms were able to initiate ahead of the boundary and primarily provided brief periods of heavy rainfall and gusty winds.  However, the only severe report that came out of the storms was a single 1 hail report and a few other sub-severe hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported nickel to quarter sized hail in Ringsted.  Delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains caused the Mississippi River to flood at McGregor (Clayton County) starting in late September. This flooding ended in early October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks at McGregor. The flooding began in late September and ended on October 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds in part of northwest Iowa shortly after midnight on the morning of July 17th. Damage reports were received from Lyon and Buena Vista Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew 3 empty grain silos off their foundations, and caused tree damage, including trees uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds in part of northwest Iowa shortly after midnight on the morning of July 17th. Damage reports were received from Lyon and Buena Vista Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged trees, crops, and a machine shed. The amount of damage was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains in late October pushed the Cedar River out of its banks at both Osage (Mitchell County) and Charles City (Floyd County). The flooding occurred on the 27th of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Cedar River out of its banks near Osage. The river crested less than a foot over the flood stage at 22.83 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Cedar River out of its banks in Charles City. The river crested just over the flood stage at 12.01 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with locally heavy rain moved across portions of northeast Iowa throughout the day on July 23rd. The heavy rain caused the Upper Iowa River to send water over County Road W14 near Kendallville (Winneshiek County). The Paradise Valley Campground (Clayton County) sustained extensive damage and had to be evacuated from flooding along a branch of the Sny Magill Creek. Buildings and roads were damaged with picnic tables destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rains pushed the Upper Iowa River out of its banks sending water over County Road W14 east of Kendallville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mini-supercells developed in a narrow corridor of weak surface based instability and   effective helicities around 50-100 m2/s2.  These parameters would typically be considered quite weak for Iowa but given it was the end of November, these values were decent and assisted by good dynamics.  Five tornadoes occurred during the afternoon, many with intermittent tracks but with plenty of spotter reports and visual evidence to substatiate their occurrence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weak tornado developed on the north side of Radcliffe doing minor damage and tipping an old truck on its side.  The tornado continued on an intermittent track across rural areas northeast of Radcliffe for several miles with little additional damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed in far northern Grundy County in rural areas southwest of Parkersburg.  The tornado was documented by video as it traveled northeast.  The funnel was diffuse and intermittent for much of it's track.  The tornado crossed into Butler County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved moved out of northeast Kansas and progressed northeast up through Iowa during the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th.  On the backside, a strong pressure gradient developed with the aide of high pressure slowly moving into the Plains off the Rockies.  As a result, a number of areas saw wind gusts in excess of 40 mph and sustained winds in excess of 25 mph.  Northern Iowa saw the strongest winds, including an automated report of 59 mph.  Additionally, tree damage was reported in central Iowa, just southwest of the Des Moines metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estherville ASOS recorded a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record to near record heavy rainfall in North Central and Northeast Iowa from 21 to 23 September of 4 to over 10 plus inches caused major river flooding on many forecast points on the Iowa tributaries of the Wapsipinicon, Cedar, lower Iowa, and Maquoketa rivers from late September through early October. Then major river flooding occurred downstream on the Mississippi River from the Iowa River and Mississippi River convergence and south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 4 to over 10 inches over significant areas from 21 to 23 September in extreme North Central and Northeast Iowa moved downstream|causing the Cedar River to rise above major flood stage levels. The Cedar River at|Conesville rose above its major flood stage level of 16.5 feet on September 28th, 2016 at approximately 5 AM CST. It crested around 18.2 feet at|approximately 9 AM CST on September 29th, 2016. It fell below major flood stage on October 1st around 11 pm CST.  For damage information please see September Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds, which were slowly decreasing after a snowfall, combined with bitterly cold arctic air to drive wind chills to 35 to 50 degrees below zero from late on December 17th through the morning of December 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds on December 17th, decreasing to around 10 mph during the night of the 17th, combined with falling temperatures to lower wind chill readings to 40 to 50 below zero. Actual temperatures dropped to colder than 20 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds developed over northwest Iowa on Christmas night and continued into the morning of December 26th. The southwest to west winds developed as a low pressure system, which had brought rain and thunderstorms on Christmas Day, moved just north of the area. Sustained winds reached up to 45 mph, and gusts were measured as high as 63 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a westerly direction followed a low pressure system which had brought Christmas Day rain and thunderstorms. There were several reports of power lines and branches down and resulting power outages, and traffic lights and some signs were damaged. The winds included estimated sustained winds of 40 mph and gusts to near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Not horribly dissimilar to the December 10-11 snowfall, low pressure moved off the southern Rockies and out into the plains.  The result was warm air advection, increased moisture, and general lift over portions of Iowa.  Snow primarily began early in the afternoon and focused on the northern two tiers of Iowa through the afternoon.  Much of the snowfall ended by the mid-evening hours, around 9 pm.  Total snowfall amounts across northern Iowa were generally in the 4 to 6 inch range, tapering off south of the northern two tiers of counties. The storm continued into Saturday as north to northwest winds picked up to around 20 to 25 mph with higher gusts to around 35 mph. This causes areas of blowing and drifting snow reducing visibilities through much of Saturday morning into the early afternoon hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a westerly direction followed a low pressure system which had brought Christmas Day rain and thunderstorms. Traffic lights and some signs were damaged. The winds included a measured gust of 59 mph at Sheldon Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracked across the Midwest on December 10th and 11th. This storm dropped a band of 3 to 6 inches of snow on portions of northeast Iowa. Despite the snow being light and fluffy, there was not much wind with the storm, so the main impact was snow covered roads. The highest reported totals were 6.3 inches near Cresco (Howard County) and 6 inches in Decorah (Winneshiek County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across Howard County. The highest reported total was 6.3 inches near Cresco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some light to moderate snowfall the previous few days, Canadian high pressure moved southward into the Plains.  As the high pressure system progressed, surface winds increased over areas of Minnesota and Iowa, quickly ushering in colder temperatures and quickly falling wind chills.  By the afternoon and overnight hours of the 17th into the 18th, wind chills plummeted well below zero, approaching the negative 30 and 40 ranges through large portions of northern and central Iowa.

The coldest air of the winter season flooded into the region in the wake of an arctic cold front on the morning December 18th. Clouds cleared from west to east as high pressure moved into the region and winds diminished to 5 too 15 mph by sunrise. Low temperatures in eastern Iowa on Sunday morning ranged from -8F degrees at Keokuk to -17F degrees at Independence and -16F at Cedar Rapids. The lowest wind chills overnight ranged from -20 to -40 across eastern Iowa. These conditions resulted in the death of a man from exposure in Cedar Rapids.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported that a car driven by a 39 year old man slid 20 to 30 yards off the road in Cedar Rapids Sunday morning. He got out of his car and slipped on ice.  Law enforcement found him lying outside of his car at 748 AM CST Sunday with severe hypotherma.  It was unclear how long he had been there. He died shortly after arriving at the hospital. The air temperature when the main was found was -15F with a wind speed of 13 knots  that resulted in a wind chill of -39. This was the 752 AM observation from Cedar Rapids Airport (KCID).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds developed over northwest Iowa on Christmas night and continued into the morning of December 26th. The southwest to west winds developed as a low pressure system, which had brought rain and thunderstorms on Christmas Day, moved just north of the area. Sustained winds reached up to 45 mph, and gusts were measured as high as 63 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a westerly direction followed a low pressure system which had brought Christmas Day rain and thunderstorms. There were several reports of power lines, branches, and a few large limbs down, and resulting power outages. At least 1000 electric customers were temporarily without power. Traffic lights and some signs were damaged. The winds included a measured gust of 63 mph at Orange City Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of snow fell across northeast Iowa from December 16th into the 17th. Accumulations generally ranged from 4 to 7 inches. As the snow was ending, northwest winds increased into the 15 to 25 mph ranged that produced plenty of drifting snow along with some visibility reductions from blowing snow. The highest reported snowfall amount was 7 inches in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County). Once the snow ended, cold air poured into the region and when combined with the winds, produced dangerously cold wind chills. Values of 25 to 40 below zero were common on the morning of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across Floyd County. As the snow was ending on the 17th, northwest winds increased to 15 to 25 mph and produced drifting snow and some visibility reductions from blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some light to moderate snowfall the previous few days, Canadian high pressure moved southward into the Plains. By the afternoon and overnight hours of the 17th into the 18th, wind chills plummeted well below zero, approaching the negative 30 and 40 ranges through large portions of northern and central Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving southward during the day on the 2nd brought strong, gusty northwest winds to eastern Iowa. Sustained wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts as high as 60 mph caused damage to trees, power lines, and personal property.  The winds were the strongest from 7AM to 7PM CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement in Clinton County reported that a small shed was blown down near Lost Nation.  The AWOS at the Clinton Airport reported a gust of 49 knots at 756 AM CST. Wind gusts between 40 and 49 knots were recorded at the Clinton AWOS between the 756 AM observation and 1256 PM observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record to near record heavy rainfall in North Central and Northeast Iowa from 21 to 23 September of 4 to over 10 plus inches caused major river flooding on many forecast points on the Iowa tributaries of the Wapsipinicon, Cedar, lower Iowa, and Maquoketa rivers from late September through early October. Then major river flooding occurred downstream on the Mississippi River from the Iowa River and Mississippi River convergence and south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 4 to over 10 inches over significant areas from 21 to 23 September in extreme North Central and Northeast Iowa moved downstream|causing the Iowa River to rise above major flood stage levels. The Iowa River at |Wapello rose above its major flood stage level of 25.0 feet on September 29th, 2016 at approximately 12 AM CST. It crested around 27.2 feet at|approximately 9 AM CST on September 30th, 2016. It fell below major flood stage on October 2nd around 1 PM CST.  For damage information please see September Storm Data.

A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms, with frequent lightning, a brief tornado, and heavy rain moved across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois the afternoon and early evening of June 14, 2016. This produced flash flooding over northeast Iowa and northwest Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management confirmed that a brief tornado touchdown occurred in a field. No damage occurred. Emergency manager confirmed video taken of the tornado shows the location and full duration of just less than 30 seconds before lifting. This tornado was seen by a large area that afternoon and was reported  by numerous funnel cloud reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance produced isolated afternoon thunderstorms with penny to nickel sized hail the main threat.
An isolated strong storm produced penny sized hail as a cluster of rain and thunderstorms moved through southeast Iowa and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a large amount of penny to near quarter size hail. Hail was less than 1 inch in diameter according to the spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure brought strong thunderstorms over Iowa and Illinois the evening of October 6th, 2016. Just ahead of the low's path, a warm front was positioned over Iowa and Illinois near Interstate 80. This allowed for winds supporting tornadoes to develop with the strongest storms. A cluster of strong to severe storms moved from near Ottumwa Iowa, northeast through Scott county, with several areas of damaging winds and tornadoes along the path from Louisa and Muscatine counties, through Scott county, and into portions of Rock Island county, Illinois before ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado occurred with a super cell thunderstorm as it passed just north of the city of Muscatine, Iowa. The tornado mainly damaged crops along its path, but also produced minor damage to two outbuildings at a hog farm. In addition, several hardwood tree trunks were snapped or uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure brought strong thunderstorms over Iowa and Illinois the evening of October 6th, 2016. Just ahead of the low's path, a warm front was positioned over Iowa and Illinois near Interstate 80. This allowed for winds supporting tornadoes to develop with the strongest storms. A cluster of strong to severe storms moved from near Ottumwa Iowa, northeast through Scott county, with several areas of damaging winds and tornadoes along the path from Louisa and Muscatine counties, through Scott county, and into portions of Rock Island county, Illinois before ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado began in southwestern Davenport in the downtown area, tracking northeast. Damage began on Credit Island, and extended through downtown Davenport, the Village of East Davenport, Bettendorf, and into rural northeast Scott County. The tornado crossed the Mississippi River south of Princeton, Iowa and continued into Illinois, hitting Cordova, Illinois before lifting. Along the path, damage was primarily to trees and outbuildings. Some trees fell on homes and cars. In downtown Davenport, the roof of the jail and the roof of a homeless shelter were also damaged. Peak winds were estimated to be 100 mph.  The widest portion of the path occurred in eastern Davenport/Village of East Davenport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense cyclone moved through the central and northern Plains on Christmas. This brought unusual weather to eastern Nebraska and west central Iowa for late December. Strong southerly winds ahead of the system brought unseasonably warm and moist air northward into the region allowing for non-severe thunderstorms to cross the area early in the day on Christmas. By afternoon, a strong front swept across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa bringing a line of strong thunderstorms. A couple of the thunderstorms were strong enough to produce isolated severe weather with damaging winds. After the passage of the thunderstorms and as the low pressure moved into South Dakota a strong cold front swept through the area with very strong damaging non-thunderstorm winds across northeast Nebraska in west central Iowa. This brought widespread tree and power line damage, as well as some minor structural damage to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer indicated that strong winds produced tree damage across mainly the northern half of the county. Winds were estimated near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds, which were slowly decreasing after a snowfall, combined with bitterly cold arctic air to drive wind chills to 35 to 50 degrees below zero from late on December 17th through the morning of December 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds on December 17th, decreasing to around 10 mph during the night of the 17th, combined with falling temperatures to lower wind chill readings to 35 to 45 below zero. Actual temperatures dropped to colder than 20 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some light to moderate snowfall the previous few days, Canadian high pressure moved southward into the Plains.  As the high pressure system progressed, surface winds increased over areas of Minnesota and Iowa, quickly ushering in colder temperatures and quickly falling wind chills.  By the afternoon and overnight hours of the 17th into the 18th, wind chills plummeted well below zero, approaching the negative 30 and 40 ranges through large portions of northern and central Iowa.

After some light to moderate snowfall the previous few days, Canadian high pressure moved southward into the Plains.  As the high pressure system progressed, surface winds increased over areas of Minnesota and Iowa, quickly ushering in colder temperatures and quickly falling wind chills.  By the afternoon and overnight hours of the 17th into the 18th, wind chills plummeted well below zero, approaching the negative 30 and 40 ranges through large portions of northern and central Iowa.
A weak cool front produced afternoon showers and thunderstorms with an isolated, brief tornado reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A traveler spotted a funnel going up and down which briefly touched down in a field with dirt debris about 2  miles northeast of Elvira. This report was relayed by the Clinton County Law Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area spawning numerous severe thunderstorms with damaging winds during the afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure brought strong thunderstorms over Iowa and Illinois the evening of October 6th, 2016. Just ahead of the low's path, a warm front was positioned over Iowa and Illinois near Interstate 80. This allowed for winds supporting tornadoes to develop with the strongest storms. A cluster of strong to severe storms moved from near Ottumwa Iowa, northeast through Scott county, with several areas of damaging winds and tornadoes along the path from Louisa and Muscatine counties, through Scott county, and into portions of Rock Island county, Illinois before ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey team indicated hardwood trees uprooted due to straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 5 to 8 inches in northwest Iowa near the northern border of the state, with lesser snow accumulations to the south. The snow fell during the day and nighttime hours of December 16th, continuing into the morning of December 17th. Generally light winds at the start of the snowfall increased to northerly at 15 to 20 mph sustained with gusts of 25 to 30 mph during the night. The winds and falling temperatures caused wind chills to drop to dangerous levels of the teens to 20 below zero while snow was falling. The winds also caused drifting snow and areas of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 8 inches, including 7.3 inches near Rock Rapids. Northerly winds increased to near 20 mph sustained with gusts near 30 mph, causing drifting snow and areas of blowing snow. The winds and falling temperatures caused wind chills to drop to near 20 below zero during the storm. Some schools were dismissed early on December 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two day period of heat and humidity affected northeast Iowa in the middle of July. Heat indices on the 21st and 22nd topped out between 105 and 115. The highest reported heat index was 120 from a mesonet reporting station in the Yellow River State Park (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices topped out between 105 and 115 on both the 21st and 22nd. The highest reported het index was 120 from a mesonet reporting station in the Yellow River State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 1 to 4 inches in far northwest Iowa. Northwest winds gusting as high as 50 mph combined with the falling snow to lower visibilities to around a half mile at times. Wind chills were driven down into the teens to lower 20s, not an unusual level for mid November. However, the change to colder weather was sudden, as unseasonably warm weather had brought daytime temperatures as warm as the 60s and 70s within the previous five days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 1 to 4 inches, including 2 inches at Rock Rapids, with up to 4 inches over the northwestern corner of Lyon County. The snow was accompanied by northwest winds gusting to 50 mph, which caused areas of visibilities around a half mile at times while the snow was falling. The low visibilities and slippery surfaces made travel hazardous. Wind chills were driven down into the teens after an immediately preceding period of much above normal temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with locally heavy rain moved across portions of northeast Iowa throughout the day on July 23rd. The heavy rain caused the Upper Iowa River to send water over County Road W14 near Kendallville (Winneshiek County). The Paradise Valley Campground (Clayton County) sustained extensive damage and had to be evacuated from flooding along a branch of the Sny Magill Creek. Buildings and roads were damaged with picnic tables destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain pushed a branch of the Sny Magill Creek out of its banks. The flooding caused the Paradise Valley Campground to be evacuated. The campground was extensively damaged, with picnic tables destroyed, gazebos washed away, a small building moved several feet, roads damaged and there was two feet of standing water in the office building.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices topped out between 105 and 115 on both the 21st and 22nd. The highest reported heat index was 117 from a mesonet reporting station in Fayette.  Oelwein had a heat index of 113 on the 21st.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches, including 3.0 inches near Everly. Winds were moderate, gusting at times to around 20 mph, and temperatures were well below freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 5 to 8 inches in northwest Iowa near the northern border of the state, with lesser snow accumulations to the south. The snow fell during the day and nighttime hours of December 16th, continuing into the morning of December 17th. Generally light winds at the start of the snowfall increased to northerly at 15 to 20 mph sustained with gusts of 25 to 30 mph during the night. The winds and falling temperatures caused wind chills to drop to dangerous levels of the teens to 20 below zero while snow was falling. The winds also caused drifting snow and areas of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 7 inches, including 6.5 inches near Lake Park. Northerly winds increased to near 20 mph sustained with gusts near 30 mph, causing drifting snow and areas of blowing snow. The winds and falling temperatures caused wind chills to drop to near 20 below zero during the storm. Some schools were dismissed early on December 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of July 7th across portions of northeast Iowa. These storms formed along a nearly stationary front that was in place across the region. The storms produced wind damage in Osage (Mitchell County), New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and Decorah (Winneshiek County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree branches were blown down in New Hampton.
